responses.strategic_rationale: Establishing a residential parasomnia unit demands rapid validation of a novel monitoring paradigm while safeguarding participant safety, data integrity, and fiscal responsibility. The chosen levers directly confront the core tensions between event yield, operational cost, staffing sustainability, and regulatory compliance. By refining recruitment focus, staffing architecture, acquisition tiering, triage automation, expansion timing, and data governance, the unit can achieve high‑quality longitudinal phenotyping, efficient annotation, and scalable enrollment within the 36‑month success criteria. These levers collectively enable the project to balance scientific rigor with practical feasibility, positioning it for future funding and broader impact.

levers.name: Recruitment Yield Calibration
levers.consequences: Targeting high‑frequency self‑reporters reduces the number of participants but increases event capture, lowering annotation burden. Narrower criteria may limit generalizability and risk selection bias, potentially affecting the representativeness of phenotypic findings.
options: Focus recruitment on participants who report at least two parasomnia episodes per month to maximize event capture.
Leverage partner neurologists for pre‑screening to identify high‑risk patients and streamline enrollment and reduce screening time.
Implement community outreach via sleep disorder support groups to broaden reach while maintaining event likelihood.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: balancing high event yield against sample representativeness. Options overlook the cost per participant and potential recruitment delays from pre‑screening.

levers.name: Night Shift Staffing Architecture
levers.consequences: Adjusting staffing model influences safety, coverage, and cost. Full‑time technicians increase reliability but raise payroll; cross‑trained daytime staff reduce cost but may increase fatigue; flexible rotation may improve morale but risk coverage gaps. Each model affects the ability to respond to escalation nights and annotation throughput.
options: Deploy dedicated full‑time night technicians to ensure consistent coverage and rapid event response and reduce overtime.
Use cross‑trained daytime staff for night coverage, rotating shifts to reduce overtime and maintain expertise.
Adopt a flexible shift rotation with overtime incentives to balance workload and maintain high morale.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: staffing cost versus safety and annotation speed. Options ignore regulatory staffing ratios and technician burnout risks.

levers.name: Tiered Monitoring Protocol
levers.consequences: Changing acquisition strategy affects data resolution, participant burden, and storage costs. Baseline dry EEG for all nights reduces burden but may miss subtle events; full PSG only on flagged nights saves resources but risks missing unflagged events; intermittent high‑resolution bursts may capture more events but increase data volume. Each choice trades off data quality against operational feasibility.
options: Maintain baseline dry EEG headbands for all nights, supplementing with full PSG only on flagged escalation nights.
Schedule full PSG on the first, mid‑stay, and final nights for every participant to capture a representative sample of events.
Introduce intermittent high‑resolution sensor bursts during peak sleep periods to increase event detection without full PSG.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: data richness versus participant comfort and cost. Options miss sensor fatigue effects and real‑time synchronization needs.

levers.name: Event Triage Automation Path
levers.consequences: Implementing semi‑automated triage reduces manual review time and may improve consistency. A rule‑based system offers transparency but limited sensitivity; a machine learning model may improve detection but requires training data; a hybrid approach balances speed and accuracy. Each path influences annotation workload, cost, and validation timelines.
options: Develop a rule‑based scoring algorithm that flags potential parasomnia events based on amplitude and timing thresholds.
Train a machine learning model on annotated data to predict event likelihood and prioritize review.
Combine human‑in‑the‑loop triage with automated pre‑filtering to reduce manual workload while retaining expert oversight and improve consistency.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: speed of review versus accuracy of event detection. Options neglect continuous model retraining and overfitting risks.

levers.name: Expansion Readiness Threshold
levers.consequences: Deciding when to expand suites affects capacity, cost, and data quality. Immediate expansion increases throughput but may dilute staffing focus; delayed expansion ensures pilot success but limits enrollment; temporary portable suites offer flexibility but may compromise data consistency. Each choice impacts the ability to meet enrollment targets and maintain safety.
options: Expand to 12 suites immediately to maximize enrollment and data collection while maintaining safety standards.
Delay expansion until 70% of participants yield at least one adjudicated event, ensuring pilot success.
Add temporary portable suites for overflow during peak recruitment periods, maintaining core suite quality and data integrity.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: scaling speed versus ensuring methodological rigor. Options fail to account for regulatory approvals and new staff training.

levers.name: Data Dissemination Governance
levers.consequences: Choosing data sharing policy influences collaboration, privacy compliance, and publication timelines. Limited sharing of de‑identified sensor data protects privacy but may slow external validation; controlled access to video data under strict agreements expands collaboration but increases administrative burden; a secure data enclave offers a middle ground. Each policy affects the project's openness and potential for secondary analyses.
options: Share only de‑identified sensor data with external researchers under data use agreements to facilitate collaboration.
Provide controlled access to video data under strict confidentiality agreements for approved collaborators and ensure compliance.
Create a secure data enclave that allows vetted researchers to access anonymized datasets with audit trails.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: openness versus privacy and compliance. Options overlook administrative overhead of secure access and potential data misuse.

responses.strategic_rationale: The Bonn residential parasomnia unit must reconcile high‑quality, longitudinal data capture with limited physical capacity, staffing constraints, and regulatory oversight. Core tensions arise between participant safety, data integrity, ethical compliance, resource flexibility, participant experience, and cohort retention. The chosen levers target these intersections by tightening safety protocols, enforcing rigorous data checks, instituting a cadence of ethics monitoring, enabling dynamic resource allocation, enhancing participant comfort, and sustaining longitudinal engagement. Together they create a balanced framework that supports rigorous phenotyping while preserving operational feasibility and safeguarding participant welfare.

levers.name: Participant Safety Protocol
levers.consequences: Implementing a robust safety protocol will reduce the risk of participant injury and increase confidence among stakeholders. However, it may increase operational complexity and require additional training hours, potentially impacting staffing flexibility.
options: Implement a staggered safety check schedule that aligns with participant arrival times to ensure compliance with safety regulations.
Implement a continuous safety monitoring protocol that integrates sensor alerts to detect potential hazards in real time.
Implement a community volunteer safety liaison that engages local residents to monitor the facility during off-hours.
levers.review_lever: The core tension is balancing rigorous safety oversight with operational efficiency. The options overlook the potential for increased staff fatigue due to continuous monitoring and the need for clear escalation pathways during emergencies.

levers.name: Data Integrity Assurance
levers.consequences: Ensuring data integrity through rigorous validation steps will improve the reliability of longitudinal analyses and support publication credibility. Yet, it may slow data ingestion pipelines and increase storage costs due to redundant backups.
options: Implement a multi-tiered data validation workflow that cross-checks sensor outputs against reference standards to guarantee accuracy before storage.
Implement a real-time anomaly detection system that flags outlier readings for immediate review to prevent downstream analysis errors.
Implement a decentralized data audit trail that records every write operation with cryptographic hashes to ensure traceability.
levers.review_lever: The core tension is between data fidelity and processing speed. The options miss the challenge of balancing audit overhead with real-time analysis demands, potentially delaying event triage.

levers.name: Ethics Compliance Cadence
levers.consequences: Establishing a structured ethics compliance cadence will streamline IRB approvals and maintain participant trust. However, it may introduce bureaucratic delays that postpone study initiation and increase administrative workload.
options: Implement a quarterly ethics audit that reviews consent procedures and data handling practices to align with regulatory expectations.
Implement a real-time ethics monitoring dashboard that tracks protocol deviations and flags them for immediate remediation.
Implement a participant feedback loop that solicits ethical concerns after each stay and integrates responses into protocol updates.
levers.review_lever: The core tension lies in maintaining rigorous ethical oversight while avoiding administrative bottlenecks. The options overlook the need for a clear escalation path when emergent ethical issues arise during participant stays.

levers.name: Resource Allocation Flexibility
levers.consequences: Adopting flexible resource allocation will allow the unit to respond to fluctuating participant loads and equipment demands. Yet, it may lead to underutilized assets during low-activity periods, increasing per-participant costs.
options: Implement a dynamic staffing model that adjusts technician shifts based on real-time occupancy metrics to match workload.
Implement a modular equipment pool that can be reallocated between suites as demand shifts to maximize utilization.
Implement a cross-training program that equips staff with multiple skill sets to cover diverse monitoring tasks.
levers.review_lever: The core tension is balancing operational flexibility with consistent expertise. The options fail to address the risk of skill dilution when staff are spread across too many roles.

levers.name: Participant Experience Enhancement
levers.consequences: Improving participant experience will increase recruitment rates and data quality by fostering comfort and compliance. However, it may require additional investment in amenities and staff time, potentially straining the budget.
options: Implement a personalized orientation program that familiarizes participants with the suite layout and monitoring equipment.
Implement a nightly wellness check that offers participants a brief survey on sleep quality and comfort.
Implement a community engagement initiative that invites local volunteers to share cultural activities during daytime hours.
levers.review_lever: The core tension is between enhancing participant comfort and maintaining research rigor. The options overlook the potential for increased noise or distraction during daytime activities that could confound physiological recordings.

levers.name: Longitudinal Cohort Cohesion
levers.consequences: Maintaining cohort cohesion over the study duration will improve longitudinal data consistency and reduce attrition. Yet, it may necessitate frequent follow-up interactions that consume staff time and resources.
options: Implement a structured follow-up schedule that sends automated reminders and offers flexible rescheduling options to maintain engagement.
Implement a cohort analytics dashboard that visualizes individual progress and flags participants at risk of dropout.
Implement a peer support network that connects participants across stays to share experiences and coping strategies.
levers.review_lever: The core tension is sustaining participant engagement while avoiding overburdening staff. The options miss the challenge of ensuring that peer support does not inadvertently influence participant behavior or introduce bias.

responses.strategic_rationale: The project balances high data fidelity with operational sustainability in a residential setting. These levers target core tensions—occupancy versus maintenance, participant motivation versus sample bias, annotation speed versus quality, synchronization precision versus computational load, safety monitoring versus naturalistic behavior, control matching versus recruitment speed, and budget timing versus flexibility. By addressing each lever, the plan navigates the trade-offs between cost, throughput, data integrity, and participant experience, ensuring that the unit can deliver robust longitudinal phenotyping while remaining financially and ethically viable.

levers.name: Sleep Suite Utilization
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever increases occupancy rates of the 8 active suites, reducing idle nights and improving cost efficiency. However, higher utilization may compress turnaround time for cleaning and equipment checks, potentially raising the risk of cross-contamination or equipment wear if not managed.
options: Implement a staggered admission schedule that aligns participant arrival times with suite turnover windows to maintain high occupancy while preserving thorough cleaning protocols.
Introduce a real-time occupancy dashboard that alerts staff to impending suite vacancies, enabling proactive scheduling of maintenance and participant onboarding.
Deploy a rotating suite assignment algorithm that balances participant exposure to different environmental conditions, reducing habituation while sustaining high utilization.
levers.review_lever: Balancing suite occupancy against maintenance rigor creates a tension between cost efficiency and equipment longevity; the options overlook the need for a formal risk assessment of accelerated wear on dry-electrode headbands under continuous use.

levers.name: Participant Retention Incentive
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever aims to reduce dropout rates during the 2–8 week stay, improving data completeness. However, increased incentives may bias the sample toward participants motivated by compensation rather than clinical relevance, potentially affecting generalizability.
options: Offer a tiered compensation structure that rewards participants for completing each night of monitoring, with incremental bonuses for consecutive nights to encourage sustained stay.
Provide personalized sleep hygiene workshops during the stay, allowing participants to gain actionable insights while reinforcing their commitment to the study.
Implement a peer-support network where participants can share experiences and receive encouragement, fostering a sense of community that motivates continued participation.
levers.review_lever: Incentive schemes risk attracting participants primarily for financial gain, yet the options fail to address potential selection bias introduced by differential motivation levels across demographic groups.

levers.name: Annotation Workflow Optimization
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever streamlines the process of scoring and annotating events, reducing technician time and improving inter-rater reliability. However, automation may introduce new errors if the workflow is not rigorously validated against manual standards.
options: Introduce a two-tiered annotation system where technicians first flag potential events using a lightweight interface, then senior reviewers confirm and refine annotations in a separate session.
Adopt a batch-processing schedule that consolidates nightly recordings into daily annotation windows, allowing reviewers to compare consecutive nights for pattern consistency.
Deploy a shared annotation repository with version control, enabling real-time conflict resolution and audit trails for every change made to event labels.
levers.review_lever: Optimizing annotation speed risks compromising quality if reviewers rush; the options do not incorporate a systematic quality assurance checkpoint to catch mislabeling before final scoring.

levers.name: Data Synchronization Protocol
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever ensures all multimodal sensor streams are time-aligned accurately, improving downstream analysis fidelity. However, stricter synchronization may increase computational load and storage overhead, potentially impacting real-time processing.
options: Implement a centralized NTP server that timestamps all devices at acquisition, coupled with periodic drift checks to maintain sub-millisecond alignment across modalities.
Introduce a post-acquisition alignment script that cross-references event markers across EEG, camera, and environmental logs, correcting any temporal offsets before storage.
Deploy a lightweight time-correction middleware that buffers incoming data streams, applies real-time offset adjustments, and writes synchronized packets to the NAS.
levers.review_lever: Ensuring perfect temporal alignment is critical, yet the options overlook the need for a fail-safe mechanism that flags persistent drift beyond acceptable thresholds during long stays.

levers.name: Safety Incident Response
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever enhances the unit's ability to respond to emergencies, reducing risk to participants and staff. However, increased monitoring may raise participant anxiety and affect naturalistic behavior.
options: Establish a rapid-response protocol that includes automated alerts to on-call staff when sensor thresholds indicate potential falls or severe arousals, triggering immediate bedside checks.
Integrate a discreet wearable alert system for participants that vibrates when motion exceeds safe limits, allowing self-initiated assistance without external intervention.
Conduct monthly safety drills with staff and participants, reviewing incident logs and refining response times to maintain high readiness levels.
levers.review_lever: Improving incident response reduces risk but may alter the natural sleep environment; the options fail to quantify how increased monitoring signals affect participant sleep quality.

levers.name: Control Group Calibration
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever refines the matched control cohort, improving the validity of comparisons between parasomnia and normal nocturnal behavior. However, stricter matching criteria may limit recruitment speed and increase costs.
options: Implement a propensity-score matching algorithm that balances age, sex, BMI, and baseline sleep architecture between parasomnia participants and controls before enrollment.
Introduce a pre-screening polysomnography for controls to confirm absence of subclinical parasomnia events, ensuring a cleaner comparison group.
Deploy a dynamic control recruitment pipeline that adjusts inclusion thresholds based on real-time enrollment metrics, maintaining target sample size without compromising matching quality.
levers.review_lever: Calibrating controls enhances internal validity, yet the options ignore the potential for overmatching, which could obscure genuine inter-individual variability in sleep patterns.

levers.name: Funding Milestone Alignment
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever synchronizes budget allocations with key project milestones, ensuring financial resources are available when needed. However, rigid milestone-based funding may reduce flexibility to respond to unforeseen opportunities or delays.
options: Adopt a rolling budget review every quarter that reallocates surplus funds to high-impact activities identified in the latest progress report.
Implement a contingency reserve that automatically triggers when a milestone is delayed, allowing immediate reallocation without board approval.
Deploy a real-time financial dashboard that visualizes cash flow against upcoming milestones, enabling proactive adjustments to staffing and equipment orders.
levers.review_lever: Aligning funds to milestones ensures accountability, yet the options overlook the need for a flexible buffer that accommodates rapid scaling of participant recruitment.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     A short critical review — name the core tension, then identify a weakness the options miss.
     Examples:
     - "Switching from seasonal contract labor to year-round employees stabilizes harvest quality, but the idle-wage burden during the 5-month off-season adds a fixed cost that erases the per-unit savings unless utilization reaches year-round levels."
     - "Each additional clinical site requires its own IRB approval, site-initiation visit, and staff credentialing — a sequential overhead of 8–14 weeks per site that compounds rather than parallelizes, so doubling site count does not halve enrollment time."
     - "Pooling catastrophe risk across three coastal regions reduces expected annual loss on paper, but a single regional hurricane season can correlate all three simultaneously, turning the diversification assumption into a concentration risk at the worst possible moment."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

6. **Option Structure**
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach (at least 15 words with an action verb) — not a short label or vague aspiration
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.

Today's date:
2026-Mar-11

Project start ASAP

File 'purpose.md':
**Purpose:** business

**Purpose Detailed:** Research facility establishment, data collection, and analysis for scientific publication and grant funding.

**Topic:** Establishment of a residential research unit for the study of adult NREM parasomnias

File 'plan_type.md':
This plan requires one or more physical locations. It cannot be executed digitally.

**Explanation:** This plan *unequivocally requires* a physical location (the research unit in Bonn, Germany), physical renovations, physical equipment, in-person staffing, and physical participants. The entire research project *revolves around* physical data collection and analysis in a real-world setting. The plan also requires physical travel to the location.
